---
title: "Bootstrap & Randomization Inference"
subtitle: "Inference when the formulas run out"
---

## Overview

Every standard error in the earlier modules came from a formula derived under assumptions --- large samples, smooth functionals, correctly specified variance. But what is the standard error of a median, of an instrumental-variables ratio, of a synthetic-control gap? And what do you do when you have only twenty-one clusters and the asymptotic theory simply does not bite?

This module develops two computational answers. The **bootstrap** treats the sample as a stand-in for the population and resamples it thousands of times, reading the sampling distribution straight off the computer --- no formula required. **Randomization inference** turns the experiment's own assignment mechanism into an exact reference distribution, delivering p-values that hold in finite samples without distributional assumptions. And the **wild cluster bootstrap** rescues inference precisely where cluster-robust standard errors fail: a handful of clusters.

Together these resampling tools are the modern econometrician's safety net, used whenever the textbook formula is unavailable or untrustworthy.

## Pages in This Module

- [The Bootstrap](the-bootstrap.qmd) --- Approximating the sampling distribution by resampling.
- [Randomization Inference](randomization-inference.qmd) --- Design-based p-values from the assignment mechanism.
- [Clustered and Wild Bootstrap](cluster-bootstrap.qmd) --- Reliable inference with few clusters.
